topic: Flagship near-miss with hand-derived nonroutine object: LP versus SVAR impulse-response identification under sign restrictions and non-Gaussian cumulant labels, repairing the banked eid_lp_svar_nonequiv_upgrade failure.

open_problems.open_problem: For a fixed reduced form, characterize exactly when common-restriction LP and SVAR impulse-response sets have identical support functions after imposing the same sign, zero, and non-Gaussian cumulant-label restrictions.

source_refs.claim: LP and VAR estimators have a common population impulse-response estimand, and structural identification including sign restrictions can be performed on either representation when restrictions are the same.
source_refs.author: Plagborg-Moller and Wolf

source_refs.claim: Sign and zero restrictions in set-identified SVARs can be encoded as lower-dimensional sign restrictions with nonemptiness and sampling algorithms.
source_refs.author: Read

source_refs.claim: Adding independence to sign restrictions can reject rotations, but confidence sets may become nonconvex and disjoint.
source_refs.author: Drautzburg and Wright

source_refs.claim: Independent non-Gaussian shocks can identify SVARs without additional economic restrictions, after which such restrictions can be tested.
source_refs.author: Gourieroux, Monfort, and Renne

source_refs.excerpt: defining a common-restriction LP set with the same cumulant restriction when the non-Gaussian label is imposed
defining a common-restriction LP set R_HLP^{common}=R_CLP
Derivation reduces to one-line bookkeeping transfer R_SVAR(P) subset R_LP(P) cap C_eps(P)

open_problems.why_unsolved: Plagborg-Moller and Wolf settle the same-estimand sanity reduction only when the identifying restrictions are held common, while Read and Drautzburg-Wright show that realistic sign, zero, and independence or higher-moment restrictions create nontrivial rotation-set geometry. The prior reviewers identified the exact failure mode in the banked attempt: it compared unequal LP and SVAR restriction sets, so the claimed gap collapsed into bookkeeping rather than a common-restriction support-function frontier.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to recover PMW by comparing horizonwise LP to cumulant-restricted SVAR fails because the bank README says this reduces to one-line bookkeeping after granting the margin.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An iff support-function equality or strict-separation theorem for LP and SVAR identified sets after the LP comparator is equipped with the same common-impact and cumulant-label restrictions as the SVAR comparator.

open_problems.open_problem: Define a non-normalized sign-exposure channel that separates genuine exclusion of masquerading shocks from artifacts of unit-effect or E=1 normalization in LP/SVAR identification.

source_refs.claim: Sign-based SVAR identification is vulnerable to shock masquerading, where linear combinations of other shocks satisfy the same sign restrictions as the target shock.
source_refs.author: Wolf

source_refs.claim: Unit-effect impulse-response identified sets can be unbounded when set-identifying restrictions do not rule out zero response of the normalizing variable.
source_refs.author: Read

source_refs.claim: Independence refinements can reject many sign-restricted rotations but may need large samples or substantial non-normality to shrink confidence sets materially.
source_refs.author: Drautzburg and Wright

source_refs.excerpt: Redefine E_w^{sign} using nonredundant sign rows and a convention for empty active-positive rows
separate sign exposure from the E=1 normalization case

open_problems.why_unsolved: Wolf names the masquerading failure, Read shows normalization can make set-identified unit-effect responses ill-behaved, and Drautzburg-Wright show independence refinements can be informative but weak and nonconvex. The failed CausalSmith upgrade reached reviewer interest but was rejected because its sign channel was normalized into a tautology rather than defined through nonredundant active sign rows, slack distance, and zero-channel conventions.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to normalize sign exposure to E=1 fails because Read shows unit-effect identified sets can be unbounded and the reviewer explicitly asks to separate sign exposure from the E=1 normalization case.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp slack-distance frontier for sign exposure, with conventions for redundant or empty active sign constraints and an iff regime statement for when the sign channel vanishes or excludes masquerading rotations.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize when fourth-cumulant or non-Gaussian component labels select the same economic shock as sign restrictions, and when they instead generate a positive support-function gap between LP/sign and non-Gaussian SVAR sets.

source_refs.claim: Independent non-Gaussian SVAR shocks identify essentially unique impulse responses, subject to labeling ambiguities such as sign and ordering.
source_refs.author: Lanne, Meitz, and Saikkonen

source_refs.claim: ICA-based non-Gaussian identification estimates structural VARs without extra restrictions, with usual economic restrictions then available as testable restrictions.
source_refs.author: Gourieroux, Monfort, and Renne

source_refs.claim: Non-Gaussianity can statistically identify SVAR parameters, but statistical identification rarely supplies economic interpretation.
source_refs.author: Lanne and Luoto
source_refs.venue: Journal of Business and Economic Statistics

source_refs.claim: Higher-moment SVAR identification imposes stronger assumptions than second-order methods and raises weak-identification concerns in finite samples.
source_refs.author: Montiel Olea, Plagborg-Moller, and Qian

source_refs.excerpt: prove Assumption~\ref{ass:quant-margin} from the primitive fourth-cumulant geometry
strict support gap is assumed through A-quant-margin rather than derived from primitive fourth-cumulant geometry
no axis-b non-equivalence threshold derived in closed form

open_problems.why_unsolved: The non-Gaussian SVAR literature gives statistical component recovery, while Lanne and Luoto explicitly warn that statistical identification rarely carries economic interpretation. The parent CausalSmith proposal was downgraded for assuming, rather than deriving, the quantitative support margin from primitive fourth-cumulant geometry, so the open issue is the exact bridge between cumulant labels, sign labels, and support gaps.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to use ICA labels directly fails because Lanne-Meitz-Saikkonen and Gourieroux-Monfort-Renne identify shocks only up to sign and permutation, and the reviewer says the resulting strict gap is assumed through A-quant-margin rather than derived.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient determinant, rank, transversality, or cumulant-sign condition under which the cumulant-labeled shock equals the sign-labeled economic shock, plus a counterexample or open-class condition yielding a positive support-function gap.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive the sharp identified-response envelope when cumulant labels are weak or local-to-Gaussian and are combined with sign restrictions under a common LP/SVAR restriction set.

source_refs.claim: Higher-moment point identification places high demands on finite samples, so weak-identification issues should be prioritized.
source_refs.author: Montiel Olea, Plagborg-Moller, and Qian

source_refs.claim: Standard non-Gaussian SVAR inference can fail to cover structural functions when deviations from Gaussianity are small; locally robust inference restores coverage.
source_refs.author: Hoesch, Lee, and Mesters

source_refs.claim: Independence-refined sign sets are weak-identification robust but may need considerable non-normality to materially shrink intervals.
source_refs.author: Drautzburg and Wright

source_refs.claim: LPs and VARs estimate the same population impulse responses under unrestricted lag structures, so identification comparisons must hold restrictions fixed.
source_refs.author: Plagborg-Moller and Wolf

open_problems.why_unsolved: Published work warns that higher-moment identification becomes weak near Gaussianity and develops robust inference for SVAR-side non-Gaussian models or independence-refined sign sets. What remains open for this topic is the sharp population identified envelope when cumulant labels are treated as weak information and imposed identically on LP and SVAR representations before applying the PMW sanity reduction.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to point-identify with higher moments fails because Montiel-Olea-Plagborg-Moller-Qian emphasize finite-sample weak identification and Hoesch-Lee-Mesters show standard non-Gaussian inference fails near Gaussianity.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A weak-identification-robust sharp identified-set or confidence-set formula for impulse responses under common sign and cumulant restrictions, with a limiting clause collapsing to PMW equality when restrictions are identical and strong.

open_problems.open_problem: Define c_w(P) as a canonical extremal support-loss-to-distance-product value on a compact local comparison set, rather than as an assumed curvature, scalar multiplier, or gap-defined threshold.

source_refs.excerpt: Define c_w(P) as a canonical extremal value, such as the infimum of the support-loss-to-distance-product ratio
Upgrade Conjecture 2 from an abstract local modulus to an explicit finite-dimensional optimization program
tau_w^*(P) is redefined as a genuine finite threshold and the parent chi_w(P) reduction is algebraic

open_problems.why_unsolved: The upgrade reviewers repeatedly rejected the old threshold because it was not canonical: it appeared as assumed local curvature, a lambda times residual expression, or a quantity that could not be shown positive finite in the intended comparison regime. The missing object is a computable extremal constant whose numerator is support loss and whose denominator is a product of independently defined channel distances.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to set tau_w^*(P) equal to a multiplier times a cumulant residual fails because the reviewer says the displayed definition cannot be positive finite when LP support maximizers are not SVAR-compatible.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A finite-dimensional extremal characterization of c_w(P) with compact feasible set, observable inputs, positivity and finiteness conditions, and an algebraic collapse to the parent chi_w(P) corner.

open_problems.open_problem: Formulate the horizonwise LP relaxation so its pasting defect genuinely measures failure of separately optimized LP response rows to share one cumulant-diagonalizing SVAR rotation.

source_refs.excerpt: the pasting defect compares genuinely horizonwise LP response rows or per-horizon optimizers against a single common SVAR rotation
the pasting channel is defined as P(P)=Q_SVAR, so it is not isolated from the cumulant/SVAR constraint
The flagship mathematical work is open in Conjectures

open_problems.why_unsolved: The failed upgrade tried to split sign exposure, pasting, and cumulant labeling, but reviewers found that Q_LP already ranged over one full common rotation in some drafts. That made the pasting channel collapse into either the LP set itself or the cumulant/SVAR residual, leaving open the correct formulation where LP rows are genuinely horizonwise before being tested for one common law.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to define the LP feasible set directly over one common rotation fails because the reviewer notes that the alleged pasting channel then does not isolate a distinct bundled mechanism.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An algebraic compatibility criterion for when horizonwise LP sign/zero support maximizers paste into one common impact rotation satisfying the non-Gaussian cumulant label.

open_problems.open_problem: Construct an auditable two-shock trigonometric witness with rotation and reflection components, explicit channel values, and dominance inequalities excluding lower-residual non-support angles.

source_refs.excerpt: spelling out the rotation and reflection component quartic residuals separately rather than leaving them as r_4
add explicit dominance inequalities excluding lower-residual non-support stationary angles on both O(2) components
spell out the promised two-shock trigonometric lower bound at least symbolically

open_problems.why_unsolved: The earlier witness was treated as promising but not fully auditable: reviewers asked for explicit rotation and reflection residuals, O(2) component handling, and inequalities ruling out non-support stationary angles. Without those ingredients, the witness cannot certify which channel actually produces the support gap.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to give only the SO(2) optimizer angle fails because reviewers ask whether the reflected O(2) component requires a second finite set and dominance checks.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A closed-form two-shock trigonometric certificate listing all rotation and reflection candidates, their sign, pasting, and cumulant channel values, and the support-maximizer dominance inequalities.

literature_map:
1. Plagborg-Moller and Wolf (2021, Econometrica) is the mandatory sanity anchor: LPs and VARs share population IRF estimands under unrestricted lag structures and common identifying restrictions.
2. Li, Plagborg-Moller, and Wolf (2024, Journal of Econometrics) and Montiel Olea, Plagborg-Moller, Qian, and Wolf (2024/2025) move the LP/VAR comparison toward finite-sample bias-variance and robustness rather than new population identification.
3. Arias, Rubio-Ramirez, and Waggoner (2018, Econometrica) and Read (2022, Econometrics Journal) give the modern computational toolkit for sign/zero-restricted SVAR identified sets.
4. Gafarov, Meier, and Montiel Olea (2018, Journal of Econometrics) compute extrema over set-identified SVAR impulse-response sets, making support-function questions natural.
5. Amir-Ahmadi and Drautzburg (2021, Quantitative Economics) show qualitative ranking restrictions can sharpen sign-restricted VAR sets, but effectiveness depends on reduced-form covariance geometry.
6. Wolf (2022, AEA Papers and Proceedings) is the sign-restriction failure anchor: weak signs can allow shock masquerading and Haar-prior weighting can be driven by volatility.
7. Read (2022, RBA Research Discussion Paper) shows unit-effect normalization can create unbounded identified sets, motivating non-normalized sign-exposure objects.
8. Gourieroux, Monfort, and Renne (2017, Journal of Econometrics) and Lanne, Meitz, and Saikkonen (2017, Journal of Econometrics) establish non-Gaussian SVAR identification through independent components.
9. Lanne and Luoto (2021, JBES) relax independence toward moment restrictions and explicitly note that statistical identification rarely supplies economic interpretation.
10. Montiel Olea, Plagborg-Moller, and Qian (2022, AEA Papers and Proceedings) critique higher-moment identification as assumption-heavy and weak-identification-prone.
11. Hoesch, Lee, and Mesters (2024, Quantitative Economics) develop locally robust inference for non-Gaussian SVARs and show standard methods can fail near Gaussianity.
12. Drautzburg and Wright (2023, Journal of Econometrics) combine sign restrictions with independence tests, producing robust but sometimes weak and nonconvex/disjoint confidence sets.
13. Giacomini, Kitagawa, and Read (2022, JBES) show narrative shock-sign information can be treated as restrictions or proxies, with weak-proxy coverage problems when shock signs are known in few periods.
14. The live gap cluster is not generic LP-vs-VAR nonequivalence; it is common-restriction geometry for sign/zero plus non-Gaussian cumulant labels, with non-normalized sign exposure and weak-higher-moment regimes handled explicitly.
prior_proposal_map:
Mined CausalSmith/doc/formalization/_bank/failed/eid_lp_svar_nonequiv_upgrade, especially reviewer JSONs angle0_v1 through angle4_v3 and the failed proposal tex.
The highest-yield upgrade reviewer files were angle4_v3, angle4_v2, angle2_v2, angle2_v1, and angle3_v1; they prescribe repairs for canonical c_w(P), sign exposure, PMW common restrictions, and operational tau/frontier definitions.
Mined CausalSmith/doc/formalization/_bank/failed/eid_lp_svar_nonequiv_upgrade2 for the later eta/leakage attempt; it is useful mainly for two-shock O(2) residual formulas and dominance inequalities, but its bank README warns the flagship frontier became definitional.
Mined CausalSmith/doc/formalization/_bank/downgraded/eid_lp_svar_nonequiv_v1 for the parent chi_w(P) margin, primitive fourth-cumulant strict-gap conjecture, and two-shock witness requests.
Bank README evidence says v1 collapsed to set-inclusion bookkeeping after granting A-quant-margin; upgrade collapsed on well-posedness/sign-cone normalization and PMW sanity issues; upgrade2 collapsed as a tautological eta frontier.
No unrelated study-pipeline IV/Manski gaps were used because they do not touch the LP/SVAR cumulant/sign-restriction topic.
